walkthrough.title: Organize the Goods Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's get Jaipur set up. First, find all the circular goods tokens. There are six types: diamonds, gold, silver, cloth, spice, and leather. Sort them into six separate stacks. Each stack should be arranged with the highest value token on top and the lowest on the bottom. This is important because the first player to sell a good gets the most valuable token.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Bonus Tokens
walkthrough.text: Now find the bonus token stacks. There are three groups: the three-good bonuses, the four-good bonuses, and the five-good bonuses. Shuffle each group separately and place them face down near the goods tokens. These stay hidden until a player earns one by selling multiple goods at once.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside the Camel Token and Seals
walkthrough.text: Find the single camel token worth five rupees and place it near the bonus stacks. Also find the three Seals of Excellence, which are the large round tokens used to track who wins each round. Place these nearby as well. The first player to earn two seals wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Build the Market
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the market between the two players. Start by placing three camel cards face up in a row. Then shuffle all the remaining goods cards and camel cards together into one big deck. Draw two cards from the top of this deck and add them face up to the market row. You should now have exactly five face-up cards in the market.

walkthrough.title: Place the Draw Deck
walkthrough.text: Take the rest of the shuffled deck and place it face down next to the market row. This is the draw deck. Whenever a card is taken from the market, you will refill the empty spot from this deck. The round ends when this deck runs out or when three goods token stacks are depleted.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal five cards to each player from the draw deck. Look at your cards privately. If you received any camel cards, immediately place them face up in front of you. These form your personal camel herd and do not count toward your hand. Keep the rest as your starting hand of goods cards.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The younger player goes first, or you can choose randomly. Players will alternate taking turns throughout the round. Remember, this is a best-of-three game. The first player to win two rounds earns two Seals of Excellence and wins. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: One Action Per Turn
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one thing: either take cards from the market or sell cards from your hand. You cannot do both on the same turn. Let's go through each option.

walkthrough.title: Take a Single Goods Card
walkthrough.text: The simplest way to take cards is to pick one non-camel card from the market and add it to your hand. Then refill the empty market spot from the draw deck. Remember, your hand can never exceed seven cards. Camels in your herd do not count toward this limit.

walkthrough.title: Exchange Cards
walkthrough.text: You can also swap two or more cards from your hand or camel herd with an equal number of goods cards from the market. You must take at least one goods card from the market. You can give any combination of hand cards and camels. For example, you could give one leather and two camels to take three goods from the market. Refill any gaps from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Take All Camels
walkthrough.text: Your third option for taking cards is to grab every camel currently in the market. You must take all of them, not just some. Add them to your camel herd face up. Then refill all the empty market spots from the draw deck. This can be very powerful when three or four camels are in the market because it completely refreshes the available goods.

walkthrough.title: Selling Goods
walkthrough.text: Instead of taking cards, you can sell goods from your hand. Discard one or more cards of the same type and take one goods token from the top of that stack for each card sold. There is a minimum for precious goods: diamonds, gold, and silver require you to sell at least two cards. Cloth, spice, and leather can be sold one at a time.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Tokens
walkthrough.text: Here is where selling in bulk gets exciting. If you sell exactly three cards at once, you also draw a random bonus token from the three-good stack. Selling four earns a four-good bonus, and selling five or more earns a five-good bonus. The five-good bonuses are worth eight to ten rupees, so they can swing a round. You only get one bonus per sale, regardless of how many cards you sell above five.

walkthrough.title: Camels and the Camel Token
walkthrough.text: Camels are not goods and cannot be sold for tokens. Their main purpose is serving as flexible trading currency during exchanges. At the end of each round, the player with more camels in their herd receives the camel token worth five rupees. If both players have the same number of camels, neither player gets it.

walkthrough.title: Token Values Decrease
walkthrough.text: This is the core tension of Jaipur. Goods tokens are stacked with the highest values on top. The first player to sell diamonds grabs the seven-point tokens while the second player gets the fives. This creates urgency to sell quickly. But selling in bulk earns those lucrative bonus tokens. Do you sell two diamonds now for fourteen points, or wait to sell four for twenty plus a bonus? That decision is what makes Jaipur tick.

walkthrough.title: Round End Triggers
walkthrough.text: A round ends immediately when either of two things happens. First, if three goods token stacks are completely depleted. Second, if the draw deck runs out and a card cannot be drawn to refill the market. When either condition is met, the round stops right away.

walkthrough.title: Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: When the round ends, award the camel token to the player with more camels. Then each player adds up all their goods tokens and bonus tokens. The player with the higher total wins the round and takes a Seal of Excellence. If tied, the player with more bonus tokens wins. If still tied, the player with more goods tokens wins.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The first player to earn two Seals of Excellence wins the game. A full game is best of three rounds. Between rounds, return all tokens to their stacks, reshuffle all the cards, and set everything up fresh from scratch. The tension resets every round, so a comeback is always possible.

summary.title: One Action Per Turn
bullets: Each turn: take cards OR sell cards, not both
Players alternate turns

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Bazaar
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns of a two-player game between Player A and Player B. You will see the key decisions and common mistakes that new players make. Let's dive in.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Market
walkthrough.text: The market has been set up with three camels and two goods cards drawn from the deck. Let's say those two goods are a diamond and a cloth. Player A has been dealt five cards: two silvers, one spice, and two camels. Those two camels go straight to Player A's herd. Player B got one gold, one leather, one cloth, one diamond, and one camel. That camel joins Player B's herd.

walkthrough.title: Player A's First Turn: Take All Camels
walkthrough.text: Player A sees three camels in the market and decides to take all of them. That gives Player A a herd of five camels, which is a strong start for exchanges later. The three empty market spots are refilled from the deck. Three new goods appear in the market.

walkthrough.title: Why Camels Matter
walkthrough.text: Taking all those camels was a smart opening move. A large camel herd gives Player A flexible trading power for future exchanges. Player A is also competing for the five-rupee camel token at the end of the round. Camels do not count toward the seven-card hand limit, so hoarding them has no downside.

walkthrough.title: Player B's Turn: Take a Diamond
walkthrough.text: Player B already has a diamond in hand. They spot another diamond in the refreshed market and snag it. Now Player B has two diamonds and can sell them next turn. Diamond tokens start at seven points each, so selling first is crucial.

walkthrough.title: Player A's Turn: Exchange for Goods
walkthrough.text: Player A wants to collect diamonds too, but there are none in the market right now. Instead, Player A uses two camels to exchange for two cloth cards from the market. Now Player A has two silvers, one spice, and two cloth in hand. The market refills with two new cards from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Player B Sells Diamonds
walkthrough.text: They take the top two diamond tokens, which are both worth seven. That is fourteen rupees from just two cards. But Player B did not get a bonus token because they only sold two cards. Still, grabbing those top-value diamond tokens early is a strong play.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Selling Leather One at a Time
walkthrough.text: Player B has one leather card and decides to sell it alone for a measly four-point token. This is a classic beginner mistake. Leather tokens decrease from four to one, so the individual values are low. The real money in leather comes from selling four or five at once for bonus tokens worth four to ten rupees. Selling one leather for four points wastes the opportunity to build toward a big bulk sale.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Bulk Sell Common Goods
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple. For common goods like leather, spice, and cloth, the individual token values are low. Accumulate four or five of them and sell in bulk. The bonus tokens are where the real value lives. A five-good bonus is worth eight to ten rupees on top of the token values. Save your bulk sales for the big payoff.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Hoarding Precious Goods
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player A has collected three gold cards and is tempted to wait for a fourth or fifth. But Player B has been picking up gold too. On their next turn, Player B sells two golds and grabs the top tokens worth six each. Now when Player A finally sells, they will only get five-point tokens. Player A waited too long and lost out on the premium values.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Sell Precious Goods Early
walkthrough.text: Precious goods like diamonds, gold, and silver have steep value drop-offs. The top diamond tokens are seven while the bottom ones are five. With gold, the drop is from six to five. Do not wait to collect five precious goods for a big bonus. Sell two or three quickly while the top tokens are still available. The three-good bonus is only one to three rupees anyway, far less than the value lost by letting your opponent grab the top tokens first.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: Player A has been accumulating goods through exchanges. They now have six cards in hand and take another from the market, making seven. On the next turn, Player A wants to take a great card from the market but cannot because they are at the hand limit. Player A is forced to sell something they were not ready to sell just to make room. Meanwhile, their camels sit in the herd untouched.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Hand Size
walkthrough.text: Always keep your hand limit in mind. Seven cards fills up fast. Use exchanges with camels to swap efficiently rather than just taking single cards. If your hand is getting full, sell your bulk goods before you are forced into an awkward sale. Camels do not count toward the limit, so a large herd gives you breathing room.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check
walkthrough.text: Player B sold diamonds early for fourteen points and has been building toward a big cloth sale. Player A has a strong camel herd and is collecting spice for a bulk sale. The diamond and gold token stacks are getting low. Keep an eye on those stacks because when three are depleted, the round ends immediately.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the End Game
walkthrough.text: Player A notices that diamond, gold, and silver stacks are all nearly empty. Player A is ahead in rupees and decides to sell two silvers. This depletes the silver stack, making it the third empty stack. The round ends immediately. Player B was saving up for a huge cloth sale but never got the chance. Player A triggered the round end at the perfect time.

walkthrough.title: Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: Now we score. Player A has more camels and receives the camel token worth five rupees. Both players add up their goods tokens and bonus tokens. Player A edges ahead thanks to the camel token and smart timing. Player A earns the first Seal of Excellence.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw the key tension between selling early for top token values versus selling in bulk for bonus tokens. You watched Player A control the end game by depleting the third stack at the right moment. Remember: sell precious goods quickly, bulk sell common goods, and keep your hand size in check.

walkthrough.title: Before You Trade
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full best-of-three match, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to sell versus when to hold.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to token values and bonus ranges.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Jaipur players make. First, selling common goods one at a time instead of saving for bulk bonuses. Second, hoarding precious goods while your opponent grabs the top-value tokens. Third, ignoring camels and losing the five-rupee camel token. Fourth, filling your hand to seven cards and getting stuck. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Sell Early Versus Sell Big
walkthrough.text: The core tension in Jaipur is timing your sales. For precious goods like diamonds, gold, and silver, sell early. The top tokens are worth significantly more, and the three-good bonus is only one to three rupees. Losing a seven-point diamond token to grab a three-point bonus is a bad trade. For common goods like cloth, spice, and leather, do the opposite. Accumulate four or five and sell in bulk. The individual tokens are worth little, but the four-good and five-good bonuses are massive.

walkthrough.title: Camel Strategy
walkthrough.text: New players often underestimate camels. A strong camel herd does three things for you. First, it competes for the five-rupee camel token, which often decides close rounds. Second, camels let you make large exchanges, swapping three or four cards at once for goods you actually need. Third, taking all camels from the market refreshes it with new cards, potentially revealing exactly what you need. Take all camels when three or more are in the market.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Jaipur is a two-player game, so pay close attention to what your opponent is collecting. If they are hoarding cloth, consider selling your cloth quickly before they can complete a bulk sale. If they leave diamonds in the market, snap them up. Watch their hand size too. If they are at six or seven cards, they might be forced to sell soon, which means certain goods will become available.

walkthrough.title: Hand Management
walkthrough.text: Your seven-card hand limit is a constant constraint. Every card you take is a slot that could hold something else. Use exchanges with camels to stay efficient. If your hand is getting full, consider whether it is better to sell now at decent values or hold out and risk getting stuck. A good rule of thumb is to never go above six cards unless you plan to sell on your next turn.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Round End
walkthrough.text: One of the most important skills in Jaipur is knowing when to end the round. The round ends when three goods token stacks are depleted. If you are ahead, you can trigger this by making strategic sales that empty a third stack. If you are behind, avoid depleting stacks and buy time for your big sales. Always count the remaining tokens in each stack so you know how close the end is.

walkthrough.title: The Best-of-Three Mindset
walkthrough.text: Jaipur is a best-of-three game, so losing a round is not the end. If you lose round one, adjust your strategy. If your opponent sold precious goods quickly, try to beat them to the punch next round. If they went for bulk sales, consider racing to deplete stacks and ending the round before they can sell. Each round is a fresh start, so adapt your approach based on what worked and what did not.

walkthrough.title: Token Math Matters
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to learn the token values before you play. Diamond tokens go seven, seven, five, five, five. Gold is six, six, five, five, five. Silver is all fives. Cloth and spice drop from five to one. Leather drops from four to one. Knowing these numbers helps you calculate whether selling now or waiting is worth more. The math is always available on the token stacks, so check before every sale.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't sell common goods one at a time — save for bulk bonuses
Don't hoard precious goods — sell early for top token values
Don't ignore camels — the 5-rupee token swings close games
Don't fill your hand to 7 without a plan to sell next turn

5-good bonus (8-10) > token value differences for common goods

summary.title: Token Math Matters

Check token stacks before every sale to calculate optimal timing

walkthrough.title: The Precious Goods Rush
walkthrough.text: The most aggressive opening in Jaipur is the precious goods rush. The idea is to prioritize diamonds and gold above everything else in the first few turns. Take single diamonds or golds from the market whenever they appear, and sell as soon as you have two. Grabbing both seven-point diamond tokens on the first sale is worth fourteen rupees in two turns. Your opponent may get the bulk bonuses on common goods, but those rarely overcome a two-token lead on precious goods.

walkthrough.title: The Camel Hoard Strategy
walkthrough.text: An alternative opening focuses on building a massive camel herd. Take all camels whenever three or more appear in the market. Once your herd is at six or seven camels, you can make huge exchanges, swapping four camels for four goods in a single turn. This lets you build a hand of matched goods extremely quickly for a devastating bulk sale. You also lock in the five-rupee camel token.

walkthrough.title: Bulk Sale Optimization
walkthrough.text: For common goods, the five-good bonus is the holy grail. Five leather tokens are worth one plus one plus one plus one plus three, which totals seven rupees. But the five-good bonus adds eight to ten. That turns a seven-rupee sale into fifteen to seventeen. Always aim for five-card sales with leather, spice, and cloth. If you cannot reach five, four is acceptable because the four-good bonus is worth four to six. Selling three common goods is usually not worth the three-good bonus of one to three.

walkthrough.title: Market Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about how their actions change the market for their opponent. When you take all camels, you force a full market refresh. If the new cards are bad for your opponent, that is a bonus. When you exchange cards, you put goods into the market that your opponent might take. Be careful about putting diamonds or gold into the market unless you know your opponent cannot use them. Sometimes it is better to sell a card than to exchange it and hand your opponent a gift.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: When you are ahead, end the round as fast as possible. Count the token stacks. If two stacks are nearly depleted, plan your next sale to empty a third. Your opponent might be sitting on a hand full of cloth ready for a massive bulk sale. If you end the round before they can sell, all those cards are worthless. This is one of the strongest moves in Jaipur and it rewards players who pay attention to the token counts.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Delay
walkthrough.text: When you are behind, do the opposite. Avoid depleting token stacks. If a stack has one token left, do not sell that good. Instead, focus on building toward your own big sale. If you can pull off a five-good sale with a ten-rupee bonus, you can swing fifteen to seventeen rupees in a single turn. Buy time by taking camels or exchanging cards rather than making sales that push you toward the round end.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Hand Reading
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to every card your opponent takes and sells. If they took two golds in a row, they likely have three or four by now and will sell soon. If they keep taking cloth, they are building a bulk sale. Use this information to either race them or to time your round-ending moves. Also watch their camel herd. If they have six camels, they are probably planning a huge exchange next turn.

walkthrough.title: The Exchange Tempo
walkthrough.text: Exchanges are the most powerful but underused action in Jaipur. A well-timed exchange lets you swap cards you do not need for exactly the goods you want, all in one action. The key is having camels to fuel exchanges. Two camels plus one hand card for three market goods is an incredibly efficient turn. Master the exchange action and you will find yourself consistently building winning hands while your opponent takes single cards.

summary.title: The Precious Goods Rush
bullets: Prioritize diamonds and gold in the opening
Sell 2 diamonds immediately for 14 rupees (7+7)
Top-token lead on precious goods is hard to overcome

summary.title: The Camel Hoard Strategy
bullets: Build herd to 6-7 camels early
Massive exchanges: swap 4 camels for 4 goods in one turn
Locks in 5-rupee camel token

summary.title: Bulk Sale Optimization
bullets: 5-good bonus (8-10) is the highest-value play for common goods
5 leather tokens (~7 rupees) + 5-good bonus (8-10) = 15-17 total
4-good bonus (4-6) is acceptable fallback
3-good bonus (1-3) rarely worth delaying for common goods

summary.title: Opponent Hand Reading

summary.title: The Exchange Tempo

Master exchanges to build winning hands faster than single-card takes

entries.definition: The 55 cards representing tradeable goods: diamonds (6), gold (6), silver (6), cloth (8), spice (8), and leather (10). Plus 11 camel cards that are not goods.
Eleven cards in the deck that are not goods. Camels dealt to your hand go to your herd immediately. Camels cannot be sold but are used for exchanges.
Circular tokens earned by selling goods. Stacked highest value on top. First seller gets the most valuable tokens.
Face-down tokens earned for selling 3, 4, or 5 goods at once. Three stacks: 3-good (1-3 rupees), 4-good (4-6 rupees), 5-good (8-10 rupees).
A single token worth 5 rupees awarded at the end of a round to the player with more camels in their herd. If tied, no one gets it.
Three large tokens used to track round wins. The first player to earn 2 Seals wins the game.
Five face-up cards between the players. Players take goods or camels from the market on their turn. Gaps are refilled from the draw deck.
Each player may hold a maximum of 7 goods cards in hand. Camels in the herd do not count toward this limit.
Take one non-camel card from the market and add it to your hand. Refill the gap from the draw deck.
Swap 2 or more cards from your hand and/or camel herd with an equal number of goods cards from the market. Must take at least 1 goods card. Cannot take camels during exchange.
Take every camel currently in the market and add them to your herd. Must take all of them. Refill all empty spots from the deck.
Discard cards of one type from your hand and take one goods token per card from the top of that stack. Precious goods require selling at least 2.
Diamonds, gold, and silver. These require a minimum of 2 cards to sell and have the highest token values.
Cloth, spice, and leather. These can be sold 1 at a time and have lower token values, making bulk bonuses more important.
Five tokens with values 7, 7, 5, 5, 5. The most valuable goods tokens in the game.
Five tokens with values 6, 6, 5, 5, 5.
Five tokens all valued at 5.
Seven tokens with values 5, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1.
Seven tokens with values 5, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1.
Nine tokens with values 4, 3, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1.
A round ends when 3 goods token stacks are depleted or the draw deck runs out.
Your personal collection of camel cards placed face up in front of you. Camels do not count toward the 7-card hand limit.
The face-down shuffled deck of goods and camel cards. Used to refill the market when cards are taken.
The full game spans up to 3 rounds. The first player to win 2 rounds earns 2 Seals of Excellence and wins.
At round end, award camel token. Total all goods tokens and bonus tokens. Higher total wins the round. Tiebreak: most bonus tokens, then most goods tokens.
